Lecture2/02_00_Introduction.reason: Motivational paragraph introducing Lecture 2 topics (localization, Dedekind domains). Claims: none. Pure narrative overview with no precise mathematical statements.

Lecture2/02_00a_Discussion.notes: Section 2.1 body. Claims: (1) universal property of localization, (2) equivalence relation construction of S⁻¹A, (3) ι(S) ⊆ (S⁻¹A)×, (4) uniqueness of factoring map, (5) for integral domains: (a,s)~(b,t) iff at-bs=0, (6) S⁻¹A embeds in Frac(A), (7) S⊆T implies S⁻¹A ⊆ T⁻¹A, (8) ι injective when A integral domain, (9) A ⊆ S⁻¹A ⊆ Frac(A). Most are in Mathlib but formalizable.

Lecture2/02_00b_Discussion.notes: Section 2.2 body. Claims: (1) contraction φ⁻¹(b) is an A-ideal, (2) extension aB = (ab : a∈a, b∈B) for injective φ, (3) a ⊆ a^ec, (4) b^ce ⊆ b, (5) if a∩S ≠ ∅ then a^e = B, (6) when B = S⁻¹A: b^ce = b.

Lecture2/02_01_Theorem.notes: Stage 3.2 review passed. All 4 claims mapped: (1) bijection via orderIsoOfPrime, (2) forward map via comap_map_of_isPrime_disjoint, (3) inverse map via isPrime_of_isPrime_disjoint, (4) round-trip via map_comap. No definition-level sorries. No gaps.

Lecture2/02_02_Remark.notes: Remark 2.2. Claims: (1) generators of a as A-ideal also generate a^e as B-ideal, (2) every B-ideal is of form a^e, (3) A noetherian implies all localizations noetherian, (4) A PID implies all localizations PID.

Lecture2/02_02a_Discussion.notes: Localization at prime. Claims: (1) A_p = S⁻¹A for S = A\p, (2) prime ideals of A_p biject with primes of A contained in p, (3) pA_p is unique maximal ideal, (4) A_p is a local ring.

Lecture2/02_03_Remark.notes: Warning 2.3. Claims: (1) a/b ∈ A_p does not require b ∉ p (counterexample: Z, p=(3), 9/3=3/1).

Lecture2/02_05a_Discussion.notes: Section 2.3 body. Claims: (1) universal property of S⁻¹M, (2) S⁻¹M ≅ M ⊗_A S⁻¹A, (3) ι injective iff ×s injective for all s∈S, (4) for A subring of field K with M ⊆ K-vector space, M embeds in S⁻¹M.

Lecture2/02_06_Proposition.notes: Defines Submodule.localizedAtPrime (M_p in ambient V), conductor ideal, states M = ⨅_p M_p and M = ⨅_m M_m. Helper lemmas: le_localizedAtPrime, localizedAtPrime_anti, conductor_not_le. 4 sorries remain (all proof-level).

Lecture2/02_06a_Discussion.notes: Claims: (1) I_p = IA_p (module localization equals ideal extension).

Lecture2/02_07_Corollary.notes: Ideal localization intersection. Defines Ideal.localizedAtPrime, states prime and maximal versions with sorry proofs.

Lecture2/02_08_Example.notes: Z = intersection of Z_(p) in Q. Specialization of Mathlib PrimeSpectrum/MaximalSpectrum.iInf_localization_eq_bot to Z and Q. Fully proved (no sorry).

Lecture2/02_08a_Discussion.reason: Transitional paragraph + section heading '2.4 Dedekind domains'. Claims: (1) 'to prove an ideal I satisfies a property, enough to show for all localizations I_p and preserved under intersections' — vague heuristic, not a precise statement (no specific property named). No other claims.

Lecture2/02_09_Proposition.notes: Fully covered by Mathlib. IsDedekindDomainDvr ↔ IsDedekindDomain via mutual instances. Covers: DVR characterization class, both directions of the equivalence, dimension ≤ 1 and integrally closed as consequences, key Mathlib lemmas. No sorries (all recall/example pattern).

Lecture2/02_10_Definition.notes: Stage 3.2 review passed. All claims mapped: (1) IsDedekindDomain definition, (2) IsNoetherianRing component, (3) Ring.DimensionLEOne component, (4) IsIntegrallyClosed component, (5) three inferInstance examples, (6) isDedekindRing_iff equivalence. No definition-level sorries. No gaps.

Lecture2/02_11_Corollary.notes: Fully covered by Mathlib. IsPrincipalIdealRing.isDedekindDomain instance. Examples: ℤ and k[X] as Dedekind domains via EuclideanDomain → PID → Dedekind chain. No sorries.

Lecture2/02_12_Remark.notes: All 4 claims covered. PID→UFD and PID→Dedekind via inferInstance; Dedekind+UFD→PID via Mathlib theorem. Counterexamples: k[x,y] not Dedekind and ℤ[√-13] not UFD stated with sorry. 2 proof-level sorries remain.

Lecture2/02_12a_Discussion.notes: Claims: (1) if A Dedekind and L finite separable extension of Frac(A), then integral closure of A in L is Dedekind (forward reference to later proof).

Lecture2/02_12b_Discussion.reason: Section heading '2.5 Fractional ideals' and context setting: 'Throughout this subsection, A is a noetherian domain and K is its fraction field.' No mathematical claims — pure scoping statement.

Lecture2/02_13_Definition.notes: Stage 3.2 review passed. All claims mapped: (1) FractionalIdeal type, (2) IsFractional predicate, (3) fg_of_isNoetherianRing bridge, (4) coeIdeal coercion from ideals, (5) coeIdeal_le_one, (6) spanSingleton_mul_spanSingleton witness for exceeding A, (7) CommSemiring and Lattice instances. No definition-level sorries. No gaps.

Lecture2/02_13a_Discussion.notes: Claims: (1) when A noetherian, ideals = f.g. A-submodules of A, (2) every ideal is a fractional ideal, (3) fractional ideals need not be contained in A.

Lecture2/02_15_Remark.notes: Remark 2.15. Claims: (1) alternative def: A-submodule I of K with nonzero r∈A s.t. rI⊆A, (2) when A noetherian this coincides with f.g. definition.

Lecture2/02_17_Definition.notes: Fully covered by Mathlib via FractionalIdeal.spanSingleton

Lecture2/02_17a_Discussion.notes: Claims: (1) I+J = {i+j}, (2) IJ = A-module generated by {ij}, (3) I÷J = {x∈K : xJ⊆I} is a fractional ideal, (4) I÷J is not a quotient of A-modules, (5) for principal ideals (x)÷(y)=(x/y).

Lecture2/02_19_Definition.notes: Fully covered by Mathlib via FractionalIdeal.mul_inv_cancel_iff and inv machinery

Lecture2/02_20a_Discussion.notes: Claims: (1) in a Dedekind domain every nonzero fractional ideal is invertible (forward ref to next lecture), (2) this is not true in general.

Lecture2/02_21_Example.notes: Subring A = Z+2iZ defined with carrier {z : GaussianInt | Even z.im}. All 5 claims addressed. 3 sorries remain in proofs.

Lecture2/02_21a_Discussion.notes: Section 2.6 intro. Claims: (1) I invertible iff I(A÷I)=A, (2) nonzero fractional ideals form abelian monoid, (3) invertible fractional ideals form abelian group.

Lecture2/02_22_Definition.notes: Fully covered by Mathlib. Ideal group ℐ_A = (FractionalIdeal A⁰ K)ˣ. Covers: definition, CommGroup instance, group operations, and note that elements need not be ideals.

Lecture2/02_22a_Discussion.notes: Claims: (1) every nonzero principal fractional ideal is invertible, (2) product of principal fractional ideals is principal, (3) P_A is subgroup of I_A.

Lecture2/02_23_Definition.notes: Fully covered by Mathlib ClassGroup. Covers: definition as ℐ_A/𝒫_A, toPrincipalIdeal map, normal subgroup, CommGroup instance, equivalence with explicit quotient, quotient map mk, and class equality criterion.

Lecture2/02_25_Remark.notes: Remark 2.25. Claims: (1) Dedekind domain is UFD iff cl(A) trivial, (2) cl(A) measures how far from UFD. Footnote: (3) Picard group ≅ ideal class group for noetherian domains.

Backmatter/Bibliography.reason: Reference list only: 5 bibliographic entries ([1] Altman-Kleiman, [2] Atiyah-MacDonald, [3] Clark, [4] Knapp, [5] Larsen-McCarthy). No mathematical claims.
